attrs.alt: AssignableScopes hierarchy: where a custom role is visible in the role picker across management groups, subscriptions, and resource groups

content.text: Every custom role definition includes an `AssignableScopes` array that lists the scopes where the role is visible in the Azure portal's role picker and can be assigned. This is separate from the assignment scope (the `-Scope` parameter on `New-AzRoleAssignment`), which determines where the assignment takes effect and inherits.

AssignableScopes is a visibility and administration control, not a security boundary. Setting a narrow AssignableScopes prevents the role from being accidentally assigned outside the intended area, but the role's permissions are still governed by its Actions and DataActions arrays.

# AssignableScopes controls WHERE a role definition can be assigned
# It does NOT restrict what the role can do — that is Actions/DataActions
# It does NOT restrict where assignments inherit — that is the assignment Scope param

# Example: role with subscription-level AssignableScopes
$role = Get-AzRoleDefinition -Name 'Virtual Machine Operator'
$role.AssignableScopes
# Output:
# /subscriptions/prod-subscription-id

# This role is visible in the role picker at:
# - /subscriptions/prod-subscription-id          (listed scope)
# - /subscriptions/prod-subscription-id/resourceGroups/rg-app  (child)
# - Any resource under rg-app                    (child of child)

# This role is NOT visible at:
# - Root management group                        (parent)
# - /subscriptions/dev-subscription-id           (sibling, not listed)

# A role with AssignableScopes = ['/'] is visible everywhere
# All built-in roles have AssignableScopes = ['/']
AssignableScopes controls where a role CAN be assigned. The assignment -Scope controls where the role DOES take effect (with inheritance to child scopes). These are two independent concepts.

AssignableScopes accepts any valid Azure scope path: root (`/`), management group, subscription, resource group, or individual resource. Multiple entries create a union — the role is visible at all listed scopes and their children. Built-in roles use `['/']`, making them assignable everywhere.

# Valid AssignableScopes values

# Root (available everywhere in the tenant)
"/"

# Management group
"/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/corp"

# Subscription
"/subscriptions/xxxxxxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxxxxxxxxxx"

# Resource group
"/subscriptions/SUB-ID/resourceGroups/rg-app"

# Individual resource
"/subscriptions/SUB-ID/resourceGroups/rg-app/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/mystorage"

# Multiple scopes in one role (union — role visible in all listed scopes)
$role.AssignableScopes = @(
    '/subscriptions/prod-subscription-id'
    '/subscriptions/staging-subscription-id'
)

# Tip: use management groups for platform-wide custom roles
# rather than listing every subscription individually
$role.AssignableScopes = @(
    '/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/corp'
)
All management groups, subs, RGs, resources
That MG and all child subs/RGs
That subscription and all its RGs and resources
That RG and all resources inside it
Management Group Scope for Platform-Wide Roles

Custom roles used across an entire enterprise — VM operators, cost readers, network contributors — belong at management group scope. Setting AssignableScopes to a management group means the role automatically covers every subscription added to that group in the future, eliminating the need to update the role definition when new subscriptions are onboarded.

# Management group AssignableScopes for platform-wide custom roles
# All subscriptions under the MG can use the role

$role = [Microsoft.Azure.Commands.Resources.Models.Authorization.PSRoleDefinition]::new()
$role.Name = 'Platform VM Operator'
$role.Description = 'Start/restart VMs across all corp subscriptions'
$role.IsCustom = $true
$role.Actions = @(
    'Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/read'
    'Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/start/action'
    'Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/restart/action'
    'Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/deallocate/action'
    'Microsoft.Resources/subscriptions/resourceGroups/read'
    'Microsoft.Authorization/*/read'
)
$role.AssignableScopes = @(
    '/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/corp'
)
New-AzRoleDefinition -Role $role

# Assign the role at the MG level (applies to all corp subscriptions)
New-AzRoleAssignment \`
    -ObjectId 'OPS-GROUP-ID' \`
    -RoleDefinitionName 'Platform VM Operator' \`
    -Scope '/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/corp'

When a custom role is purpose-built for one team's resource group, setting AssignableScopes to that resource group prevents the role from appearing in the picker at any other scope. This guards against accidental cross-team privilege escalation: an administrator in a different subscription cannot assign this role even with User Access Administrator privileges, because the role is simply not listed.

# Narrow AssignableScopes to a resource group or specific resource
# Use when a custom role is purpose-built for one team's resource group

$role = [Microsoft.Azure.Commands.Resources.Models.Authorization.PSRoleDefinition]::new()
$role.Name = 'rg-payments Key Vault Reader'
$role.Description = 'Read Key Vault secrets — visible only in rg-payments'
$role.IsCustom = $true
$role.Actions = @('Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults/read')
$role.DataActions = @(
    'Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults/secrets/getSecret/action'
    'Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults/secrets/readMetadata/action'
)
$role.AssignableScopes = @(
    '/subscriptions/SUB/resourceGroups/rg-payments'
)
New-AzRoleDefinition -Role $role

# This role CANNOT be assigned in any other resource group or subscription
# It will not appear in the role picker at any other scope
# Helps prevent accidental cross-team access grant

# Assign within the resource group
New-AzRoleAssignment \`
    -ObjectId 'PAYMENTS-APP-SP-ID' \`
    -RoleDefinitionName 'rg-payments Key Vault Reader' \`
    -Scope '/subscriptions/SUB/resourceGroups/rg-payments/providers/Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults/payments-kv'

AssignableScopes can be extended or shrunk at any time by updating the role definition. Shrinking removes the role from the picker at removed scopes, but does not delete existing assignments at those scopes — those assignments remain active until explicitly removed.

# Extend AssignableScopes when a new subscription is onboarded
$role = Get-AzRoleDefinition -Name 'Virtual Machine Operator'
$role.AssignableScopes.Add('/subscriptions/new-subscription-id')
Set-AzRoleDefinition -Role $role

# Shrink AssignableScopes (role becomes invisible at removed scopes)
# Existing assignments at those scopes are NOT deleted — they still work
# but the role can no longer be assigned there (and won't show in picker)
$role.AssignableScopes.Remove('/subscriptions/old-subscription-id')
Set-AzRoleDefinition -Role $role

# Find all custom roles that are NOT listed in your current subscription
# (i.e., roles defined in a sibling subscription or parent MG)
$currentSub = (Get-AzContext).Subscription.Id
Get-AzRoleDefinition | Where-Object {
    $_.IsCustom -and
    -not ($_.AssignableScopes | Where-Object {
        "/subscriptions/$currentSub" -like "$_*"
    })
} | Select-Object Name, AssignableScopes

In Bicep, a role definition deployed at management group scope must include that management group's ID in AssignableScopes. The deployment scope and at least one AssignableScopes entry must overlap — deploying at `/subscriptions/SUB` with AssignableScopes set to a management group fails because the subscription is not within the management group scope hierarchy of the template.
AssignableScopes mistakes cause either deployment failures (scope mismatch) or governance gaps (role too broadly visible, or assignments orphaned after scope removal).

# Pitfall 1: Deploying role definition at a scope outside AssignableScopes
# Error: The role definition with name 'X' does not have the scope '/subscriptions/SUB'
# in its assignable scopes.
# Fix: the deployment scope must be included in AssignableScopes (or be a child)

# Pitfall 2: Listing every subscription instead of using management groups
# BAD — must update role every time a new subscription is created
$role.AssignableScopes = @(
    '/subscriptions/sub-1'
    '/subscriptions/sub-2'
    '/subscriptions/sub-3'
    # ... 40 more subscriptions
)
# GOOD — automatically covers new subscriptions added to the MG
$role.AssignableScopes = @('/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/corp')

# Pitfall 3: Confusing AssignableScopes with assignment scope
# AssignableScopes = where the role APPEARS in the picker
# Assignment -Scope = where the role TAKES EFFECT (and inherits to children)

# Example: role with AssignableScopes=['/subscriptions/prod']
# Assigned at -Scope '/subscriptions/prod/resourceGroups/rg-app'
# Effect: principal can use the role's Actions only within rg-app
# (even though AssignableScopes allows assignment at subscription level too)

# Pitfall 4: Removing a scope from AssignableScopes after assignments exist
# Existing assignments REMAIN ACTIVE — they just cannot be reassigned
# To fully remove access: delete assignments first, then update AssignableScopes
ARM deployment error at role creation
Role definition requires update for every new sub
Role visible at wrong level or access wider than expected
Assignments remain active after scope removal
Delete assignments first, then update AssignableScopes
Centralized Role Management with Management Group Scopes

In large organizations, the platform team defines custom roles once at management group scope and delegates assignment to individual business unit security teams. This pattern ensures consistent role definitions across all subscriptions while allowing decentralized assignment authority.

# Custom roles are tenant-scoped — they exist once per Entra ID tenant
# A role defined in subscription A is visible in subscription B
# IF subscription B is within the role's AssignableScopes

# Scenario: central platform team manages roles for all business unit subscriptions
# All BU subscriptions are under /providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/corp

# Step 1: Check which subscriptions are under the corp MG
Get-AzManagementGroupSubscription -GroupId 'corp' |
    Select-Object Id, DisplayName

# Step 2: Create the role at MG scope (appears in all BU subs automatically)
$role.AssignableScopes = @('/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/corp')
New-AzRoleDefinition -Role $role

# Step 3: When a new BU subscription is added to corp MG,
# the role automatically becomes available there — no role update required

# Step 4: Each BU's security team assigns the role within their own subscription
# They see the role in the picker because their sub is under corp MG

# Delegation pattern:
# Platform team: define roles (platform admin responsibility)
# BU teams: assign roles within their subscriptions (delegated by UAA)
# Result: consistent role definitions, decentralized assignment
Roles needed across the whole tenant

When a custom role needs to be assignable in multiple peer subscriptions, prefer setting AssignableScopes to their shared parent management group rather than listing each subscription explicitly. This avoids having to update the role definition each time a new subscription is added.

A custom role with AssignableScopes set to root (`/`) is visible tenant-wide — equivalent to a built-in role in terms of picker visibility. Audit custom roles regularly to identify overly broad scopes, zero-assignment roles (candidates for pruning), and roles that should be migrated to management group scope.

AssignableScopes is the administrative visibility control for custom role definitions — it determines where the role appears in the role picker and can be assigned. It is not a security constraint on what the role can do or where assignments take effect.
Set AssignableScopes to management groups for platform-wide roles to auto-cover new subscriptions.
Set AssignableScopes to a resource group for team-scoped roles to prevent accidental cross-team assignment.
The deployment scope in Bicep/ARM must be included in (or be a child of) at least one AssignableScopes entry.
Shrinking AssignableScopes removes the role from the picker but does not delete existing assignments.
AssignableScopes = where to assign. Assignment -Scope = where permissions take effect.
Built-in roles use AssignableScopes = ['/'] — visible everywhere in the tenant.
